# Reproducibility and Safety Checklist {#sec-checklist .unnumbered}

Run this pass before you circulate results, hand a project to a
collaborator, or send a job to a server. Each item points to the
chapter that explains it. The standard is the one the companion Git
book sets and this book executes: someone with only your repository can
rerun everything from scratch with a single command, and get the same
answer.

## Scripts fail loudly and reproducibly {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 11](chapters/11-scripts-fail-loudly.qmd).

- [ ] Every analysis script starts with the standard header:
  `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, `set -euo pipefail`, and, where locale- or
  time-sensitive text enters the output, `export LC_ALL=C` and
  `export TZ=UTC`.
- [ ] The shebang is `#!/usr/bin/env bash`, never `#!/bin/sh`, and the
  script avoids bash-4 features unless a modern bash is targeted (the
  macOS bash-3.2 floor).
- [ ] A failing step stops the run instead of writing a confident,
  empty result; `shellcheck` and `shfmt` have been run on the script.

## The environment is locked {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 13](chapters/13-environments.qmd).

- [ ] A real lockfile is committed (`uv.lock` for Python,
  `renv.lock` for R), together with its manifest, and the built
  environment (`.venv/`, `renv/library/`) is git-ignored.
- [ ] The code runs inside the locked environment (`uv run python ...`,
  `renv::restore()`), and `uv run --locked` is used where a stale lock
  should error rather than silently re-resolve.
- [ ] A version receipt is saved beside a run (`uv pip list`,
  `sessionInfo()`) so a puzzling result can be traced to its packages.

## Data has a defined home {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 6](chapters/06-organizing-project.qmd) and
[Chapter 12](chapters/12-make-pipeline.qmd).

- [ ] Raw data is treated as immutable: no in-place edit (`sed -i` on a
  raw file, an editor save) ever breaks the provenance chain.
- [ ] Synthetic or generated data rebuilds from a script with a fixed
  seed; licensed or restricted data is described, never committed.
- [ ] `data/clean` and `output` are regenerable cache, not committed
  artifacts; only `data/raw` and `scripts` are protected.

## Nothing secret leaks {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 10](chapters/10-processes-permissions-secrets.qmd).

- [ ] No secret is typed on a command line; keys are read from an
  environment variable or a `chmod 600` file, and a `.gitignore`
  stanza (`.env`, `*.pem`, `secrets.*`) keeps them out of Git.
- [ ] Any credential that has touched a command line, a screen, a log,
  or an agent prompt is treated as compromised and rotated, not just
  deleted.
- [ ] Nothing sensitive was pasted into an AI agent's prompt; the agent
  is pointed at the mechanism (read `API_KEY`), not the secret. See
  [Chapter 17](chapters/17-ai-terminal.qmd).

## Destructive commands are guarded {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 7](chapters/07-pipes-redirection-danger.qmd) and the
[panic reference](#sec-panic).

- [ ] The read-only form was run first on the exact target: `ls` before
  a copy, `find -print` before `-delete`, `rsync -n` before a real
  `--delete`, `make -n` before a build.
- [ ] `rm -rf` never sits beside an unguarded variable; paths are
  quoted and guarded with `${VAR:?}`.
- [ ] An installer is fetched, read, and checksummed against a hash from
  a different channel before it is run; no blind `curl | sh`. See
  [Chapter 3](chapters/03-setup.qmd).

## The pipeline reproduces end to end {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 12](chapters/12-make-pipeline.qmd) and
[Chapter 14](chapters/14-ssh-remote-compute.qmd).

- [ ] One `make` rebuilds the whole study from the seed, and
  `make check` re-derives the locked data hash and the alpha (fixed to
  four decimals, since compiled BLAS numerics differ across machines)
  and fails on any drift.
- [ ] A file transferred to or from a server is checksum-verified on
  both ends (`sha256sum` / `shasum -a 256`), so the same bytes sit on
  both machines.
- [ ] A long remote job runs in a persistent session (tmux) and writes
  its own log with `tee`, so both the result and its record outlive the
  connection. See [Chapter 15](chapters/15-persistent-sessions.qmd).

## Config survives leaving your machine {.unnumbered}

See [Chapter 18](chapters/18-startup-files-path.qmd).

- [ ] The pipeline does not depend on interactive shell config. Which
  startup file a shell reads depends on the shell and how it is
  launched: an ordinary non-interactive bash reads none unless
  `BASH_ENV` is set, though a bash started by `sshd` for
  `ssh host 'cmd'` may read `~/.bashrc`; zsh reads `~/.zshenv` on every
  invocation; a login shell also reads the profile. Because
  `ssh host 'cmd'`, cron, and CI each run with a different or reduced
  environment, probe the failing context and set what the script needs
  explicitly (`LC_ALL`, `TZ`, `PATH`) rather than trusting that a
  dotfile ran.
- [ ] Settings a shell needs everywhere go in the file that shell
  actually reads on every invocation (`~/.zshenv` for zsh; bash has no
  such universal file, so a script sets its own), and scheduled
  contexts (cron, launchd, CI) get their PATH and environment set
  explicitly rather than inherited.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
The only test that matters is the clean-clone test: clone the
repository fresh into a new directory, restore the locked environment,
run `make`, and confirm the headline result returns and `make check`
passes. If it does, the pipeline reproduces. If it does not, fix the
pipeline, not your memory of how it works. See
[Chapter 12](chapters/12-make-pipeline.qmd).
:::

See the [command cheat sheet](#sec-cheatsheet) for the commands behind
each item, and the [panic reference](#sec-panic) for recovery when a
destructive command gets past a guard.
